title: 2026 U.S. Senate race in Louisiana
description: Louisiana will elect one U.S. senator in 2026 for the Class II seat currently held by Bill Cassidy. The race matters because it will help determine control of the U.S. Senate, and Louisiana is now using closed partisan primaries and a runoff system for this office. The Republican primary is the main contest so far, with Cassidy facing challengers including John Fleming and Julia Letlow; the state is widely rated Republican-leaning, but the primary has drawn attention because of Trump and Gov. Jeff Landry’s endorsement of Letlow and Cassidy’s history of breaking with Trump on impeachment.

candidates.name: Bill Cassidy
candidates.summary: Bill Cassidy is the incumbent U.S. senator from Louisiana and a physician by training. He has emphasized his record on health care, energy, border security, disaster recovery, and conservative priorities, while also highlighting his work with President Trump on some legislation. Cassidy has served in the Senate since 2015 after earlier service in the Louisiana state Senate and U.S. House.

candidates.name: John Fleming
candidates.summary: John Fleming is a Republican candidate in the Louisiana Senate primary and, as of 2026, serves as Louisiana state treasurer. He previously represented Louisiana’s 4th Congressional District in the U.S. House and has also worked as a physician, business owner, and Navy officer. His campaign has framed him as a conservative aligned with President Trump and focused on border security, Louisiana jobs, and traditional Republican priorities.

summary_sources.title: United States Senate election in Louisiana, 2026 - Ballotpedia

career_history.title: Physician
career_history.description: Worked as a physician before and during his political career.

candidates.name: Julia Letlow
candidates.summary: Julia Letlow is a Republican U.S. representative from Louisiana’s 5th Congressional District and a candidate for the Senate seat in 2026. Before Congress, she worked in higher education and university administration, and her campaign has emphasized conservative themes such as border security, law enforcement, education, and support for President Trump. She entered Congress in 2021 after winning a special election to succeed her late husband, Luke Letlow.
